title: Time & Space Complexity
description: Mastering Big-O analysis is table stakes at FAANG. This covers complexity of all built-in Python operations, amortized analysis, space complexity, and the mental models interviewers expect.

sections.title: Big-O Complexity of Python Built-ins
sections.description:
Every FAANG interview involves reasoning about complexity. Knowing these by heart prevents costly mistakes.

Operationlistdictsetdeque
append / addO(1)*O(1)*O(1)*O(1)
insert(0, x)O(N)——O(1)
lookup / inO(N)O(1)*O(1)*O(N)
delete by indexO(N)O(1)*O(1)*O(1)
sortO(N log N)———
index accessO(1)O(1)*—O(1)

* = amortized. Worst case may differ due to hash collisions or resize.
sections.codeBlock:
# Common gotcha: list vs set membership
my_list = list(range(10_000))
my_set  = set(range(10_000))

# O(N) — scans every element
9999 in my_list   # slow

# O(1) amortized — hash lookup
9999 in my_set    # fast

# Gotcha: list.insert(0, x) is O(N) — shifts all elements
# Use collections.deque for O(1) front operations
from collections import deque
dq = deque([1, 2, 3])
dq.appendleft(0)   # O(1) — not O(N) like list

sections.title: Amortized Analysis: Dynamic Arrays
sections.description:
Python lists are dynamic arrays. When capacity is exceeded, Python allocates a new array with ~1.125× more space and copies all elements. A single append can be O(N) in the worst case, but the amortized cost over N appends is O(1) — because expensive resizes happen exponentially less often.

Why this matters in interviews: Don't confuse worst-case single-operation cost with amortized cost. The interviewer may push back if you say append is always O(1).
sections.codeBlock:
import sys

# Observe list capacity growth (CPython implementation)
a = []
sizes = []
for i in range(20):
    a.append(i)
    sizes.append(sys.getsizeof(a))

# Capacity jumps at: 0, 4, 8, 16, 25, 35, 46, 58...
# Pattern: each resize multiplies capacity by ~1.125x
# Amortized cost: each element is copied at most O(log N) times total
# Total copy work for N appends: N + N/2 + N/4 + ... = 2N → O(N) total → O(1) per append

# Proof via accounting argument:
# Each append deposits 1 'token' for itself + 1 token for future copy
# When resize happens, every element has already paid for its copy

sections.title: Recursion & Stack Space Complexity
sections.description:
Every recursive call uses O(1) stack frame space. The total space used equals the maximum recursion depth — the longest path from root to base case.

Common patterns:
• DFS on tree of height H → O(H) stack space → O(N) worst case (skewed tree)
• Divide and conquer (merge sort, quick sort) → O(log N) depth → O(log N) stack
• Tail recursion: Python does not optimize tail calls. At ~1000 frames you get RecursionError. Convert deep recursion to iterative with an explicit stack.
sections.codeBlock:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(10_000)  # extend if needed (risky for large inputs)

# Space: O(H) where H = height of recursion tree
def dfs_recursive(node):     # O(H) stack space
    if not node: return 0
    return 1 + max(dfs_recursive(node.left), dfs_recursive(node.right))

# Better: iterative avoids stack overflow
def dfs_iterative(root):     # O(H) explicit stack, no recursion limit
    if not root: return 0
    stack, max_depth = [(root, 1)], 0
    while stack:
        node, depth = stack.pop()
        max_depth = max(max_depth, depth)
        if node.left:  stack.append((node.left, depth + 1))
        if node.right: stack.append((node.right, depth + 1))
    return max_depth

sections.title: Space Complexity Patterns
sections.description:
Space complexity counts all memory used beyond the input, including auxiliary data structures, recursion stack, and output.

In-place algorithms: O(1) extra space (e.g., in-place sort, two-pointer reverse)
Single data structure: Usually O(N) — hashmap, stack, queue
2D DP tables: O(N×M) — often reducible to O(min(N,M)) by rolling rows

FAANG tip: After giving your solution, proactively say 'This uses O(N) space. I can reduce it to O(1) by...' — shows depth.
sections.codeBlock:
# DP space optimization: 2D → 1D rolling array
# Problem: Coin Change (standard DP)
def coin_change(coins, amount):
    # Naive: dp[i][j] = min coins for amount j using first i coin types → O(N*amount)
    # Optimized: 1D dp, process each coin in-place → O(amount)
    dp = [float('inf')] * (amount + 1)
    dp[0] = 0
    for coin in coins:
        for j in range(coin, amount + 1):
            dp[j] = min(dp[j], dp[j - coin] + 1)
    return dp[amount] if dp[amount] != float('inf') else -1

# LCS: O(N*M) → O(min(N,M)) by always iterating over shorter string
def lcs_optimized(s1, s2):
    if len(s1) < len(s2): s1, s2 = s2, s1  # s1 is longer
    prev = [0] * (len(s2) + 1)
    for c in s1:
        curr = [0] * (len(s2) + 1)
        for j, d in enumerate(s2, 1):
            curr[j] = prev[j-1] + 1 if c == d else max(prev[j], curr[j-1])
        prev = curr
    return prev[-1]

sections.title: Complexity of Sorting Algorithms
sections.description:
Python's list.sort() and sorted() use Timsort — a hybrid merge sort + insertion sort. It's O(N log N) worst case and O(N) on already-sorted data. Stable, in-place for lists.

AlgorithmBestAverageWorstSpaceStable
Timsort (Python)O(N)O(N log N)O(N log N)O(N)✓
Merge SortO(N log N)O(N log N)O(N log N)O(N)✓
Quick SortO(N log N)O(N log N)O(N²)O(log N)✗
Heap SortO(N log N)O(N log N)O(N log N)O(1)✗
Counting SortO(N+K)O(N+K)O(N+K)O(K)✓
sections.codeBlock:
# Custom sort key — O(N log N), key called once per element
words = ['banana', 'apple', 'fig', 'cherry']

# Sort by length, then alphabetically (stable sort preserves order within ties)
words.sort(key=lambda w: (len(w), w))
# ['fig', 'apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# Counting sort when K (range) is small — beats O(N log N)
def counting_sort(arr, k):  # arr contains integers in [0, k]
    count = [0] * (k + 1)
    for x in arr: count[x] += 1
    result = []
    for val, freq in enumerate(count):
        result.extend([val] * freq)
    return result  # O(N + K) time, O(K) space

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• timeit — Measure execution time of small code snippets — Python benchmarking tool
  • Python Time Complexity (Wiki) — Official wiki documenting complexity of built-in operations
  • Big-O Cheat Sheet — Comprehensive complexity reference for data structures and algorithms
  • CLRS — Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.) — The definitive algorithms textbook, FAANG interview standard
  • Sorting Algorithms in Python — Real Python guide with complexity analysis

details:
Hash Collision Handling: Python dicts use open addressing with pseudo-random probing. In pathological cases (many hash collisions), O(1) dict operations degrade to O(N). In practice, Python's hash randomization (enabled by default since 3.3) makes deliberate collision attacks impossible in production.

Lower Bounds: Comparison-based sorting cannot do better than O(N log N) — this is proven by the decision tree argument (N! possible orderings require at least log₂(N!) ≈ N log N comparisons). Non-comparison sorts (counting, radix, bucket) break this barrier but have constraints on input range or type.

Interview pattern: Always state time AND space complexity before the interviewer asks. Then proactively offer the trade-off: 'I can improve time from O(N²) to O(N log N) at the cost of O(N) extra space by using a sorted structure.'
